planting.note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: This Italian heirloom zucchini bears ribbed, mottled fruit with a firm texture and rich, nutty flavor. Harvest young at 4 to 8 inches for the best tenderness, or let fruit reach up to 18 inches for cooking. Pick often to keep the compact plants producing all season. The abundant male blossoms are also excellent stuffed or fried. Water evenly and give plants room for good airflow to reduce powdery mildew.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out after the last frost.

directions: A classic Italian heirloom, this indeterminate variety produces deeply ribbed, rich-red fruit of 6 to 8 ounces with an intense, old-world flavor balancing sweetness and acidity. The thick, meaty walls make it ideal for sauces and paste. Provide sturdy staking or a tall cage since vines grow long and heavy. Prune suckers for larger fruit and better airflow. Feed and water consistently to prevent blossom-end rot and cracking.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors 3 weeks early in short-season areas.

directions: This heirloom watermelon produces round, 6 to 11 pound fruit about 10 inches across with sweet, creamy pale-white flesh under a thin, delicate rind. Its relatively short season makes it a good choice for northern gardens, especially with a black plastic mulch to warm the soil. Handle the fragile rind gently at harvest. Water deeply but reduce moisture as fruit ripens to concentrate sweetness. Give vines ample space to sprawl.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after frost danger passes.

directions: This determinate variety bears cylindrical, cream to pale-yellow fruit best picked at about 3 inches when they resemble small sausages. The smooth, buttery, mild flavor is excellent eaten fresh or simmered into pale sauces. The compact bush habit suits containers and small beds and needs little staking. Because it sets fruit over a concentrated window, it is well suited to canning batches. Keep watering steady to avoid cracking.

planting.note: Sow in late summer to early fall, or in early spring, for nitrogen fixation and cover.

directions: This annual clover fixes nitrogen while its deep-red, elongated flower heads attract bees, ladybugs, and other beneficial insects. Broadcast seed onto a firm, moist seedbed and rake in lightly for good soil contact. Sow in fall in mild climates to overwinter, or in early spring in colder regions. Terminate by mowing or tilling at early bloom to return the most nitrogen to the soil. It also makes an attractive, low-maintenance living mulch.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: This large spring radish reaches 1 to 2 inches across with a crisp white interior and a mild flavor perfect for salads and sandwiches. It grows fast, making it a great choice for children and impatient gardeners. Sow directly in cool weather since heat causes it to bolt and turn pithy. Thin seedlings early to give roots room to swell. Keep soil evenly moist for tender, uncracked roots.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil, or start indoors early in short seasons.

directions: A high-quality home-garden standard, this variety weighs about 20 to 25 pounds with bright crimson, sweet flesh. With a little care it can be grown in northern climates, especially using black plastic mulch and starting seeds indoors. Provide rich, well-drained soil and consistent moisture during vine growth and fruit set. Ease off watering as melons ripen to boost sugar. Harvest when the ground spot turns creamy yellow and the tendril nearest the fruit dries.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant out around the last frost.

directions: This award-winning eschalion produces oblong, 4 to 5 inch bulbs with coppery-pink skin and rosy-purple inner rings, combining the fruity sweetness of shallots with the easy peeling of onions. Start seed indoors early since it needs a long season to size up. Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Keep beds weed-free and evenly watered until tops begin to fall, then cure bulbs in a dry, airy spot. Stored properly they keep for months.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring as soon as soil is workable; carrots resent transplanting.

directions: This late-19th-century heirloom offers strong color, high yield, and uniform, tapered roots that perform well even in heavy soils thanks to their vigorous tops. Sow seed thinly directly in the garden and keep the surface consistently moist until the slow seeds sprout. Thin seedlings to prevent crowding and forked roots. It interplants nicely with onions. Loosen and deeply cultivate the bed before sowing for the straightest roots.

planting.note: Start indoors for spring, or direct sow in mid-summer for a fall and winter crop.

directions: This extra-cold-hardy lacinato-type kale features striking purple midribs against blue-green, savoyed leaves that deepen in color during cool fall weather. It withstands frost better than most dinosaur kales, and flavor sweetens after a light freeze. Grow in full sun to partial shade in rich, moist soil. Harvest outer leaves as needed to keep plants producing. It overwinters well in mild regions for early spring greens.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant out only after nights stay warm.

directions: This blend of mini sweet snack peppers ripens to red, yellow, and orange, all prized for crunchy, juicy texture and vibrant color. Start seed indoors early since peppers need a long warm season. Harden off carefully and transplant only after soil and nights are reliably warm. Plants are productive and well suited to containers and small beds. Pick regularly once fruit colors up to encourage continued set.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall harvest.

directions: A dependable heirloom, this beet forms tender, deep-crimson roots of 2.5 to 3 inches that stay smooth rather than woody even when grown large. The nutritious greens are excellent cooked like chard or spinach. Because each seed is a cluster, thin seedlings to one per spot for well-formed roots. Sow in cool weather and keep evenly watered to prevent zoning and cracking. Perfect for roasting, pickling, or grating raw into salads.

planting.note: Start indoors for spring, or sow mid-summer for a fall crop.

directions: This Italian classic produces a modest central head followed by numerous side shoots for a long, staggered harvest. Cut the main head at about 3 inches across to encourage abundant smaller shoots. Its variable maturity makes it excellent for extended picking through summer. Grow in cool weather in rich, moist soil and full sun. Keep well watered to prevent premature bolting in warm spells.

planting.note: Sow directly after the soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.

directions: This pale, tender squash is ideal for the Middle Eastern stuffed dish koosa, thanks to its firm flesh and hollow-friendly shape. The compact plants produce continuously all summer, and large leaves shade and protect developing fruit. Harvest young and often to keep production going. Sow directly into warm, fertile soil in full sun. Provide steady moisture and good airflow to limit mildew.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors 3 weeks early and transplant carefully.

directions: Introduced in 1924, this prolific heirloom bears slender, dark-green fruit 4 to 6 inches long, ideal for pickling or fresh slicing. Frequent picking spurs even heavier production, living up to the name. Grow on a trellis to save space and keep fruit clean and straight. Sow into warm, rich soil and water consistently to avoid bitterness. Keep vines evenly moist during flowering and fruiting.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant out after nights are reliably warm.

directions: This dual-purpose chile is used mirasol-style when fresh with a fruity, mild-to-medium heat around 2,500 to 5,000 Scoville units, then becomes guajillo when dried into deep-burgundy pods. Vigorous plants yield tapered fruit of 6.5 to 8 inches with good dry weight for sauces, rubs, and powders. Start seed indoors early for a long warm season. Transplant into full sun after frost danger passes. Harvest fully ripe pods and dry them in a warm, airy place.

planting.note: Start indoors for spring, or sow mid-summer for fall and overwintering.

directions: This hardy 1800s variety forms compact, deeply curled blue-green leaves and overwinters in all but the coldest climates. It grows best in cool weather but tolerates some heat, and flavor sweetens after frost. The nutrient-dense leaves are low in calories and rich in vitamins. Harvest outer leaves regularly to keep the plant producing. Grow in full sun to partial shade in fertile, moist soil.

planting.note: Start indoors early; transplant out after nights stay warm.

directions: This jalapeno bears bold flavor in a compact 3 inch pepper and is better adapted to cooler climates than most jalapenos, making it a good choice for short-season gardens. Enjoy pods green for a mild-to-medium kick or let them ripen red for added sweetness. Start seed indoors early and transplant into full sun after the soil warms. Pick regularly to encourage continued production. Fruit sets best with warm days and consistent moisture.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once soil has warmed.

directions: This award-winning yellow straightneck squash yields thick, firm, fine-grained fruit in as little as 45 days. Versatile in the kitchen, it can be eaten raw, pickled, cooked, or baked into sweet breads. Harvest young, around 4 to 6 inches, for the best texture and to keep plants producing. Sow directly into warm, fertile soil in full sun. Keep evenly watered and pick frequently.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Grown since the early 1900s, this early variety delivers abundant, tasty greens up to 18 inches tall along with flavorful roots, and is ideal where multiple crops fit the season. The leaves are high in vitamins A and C, iron, and potassium. Since each seed is a cluster, thin seedlings for well-shaped roots. Sow in cool weather and keep soil evenly moist. Harvest roots young and tender or let them size up.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall.

directions: This colorful mix produces red, white, rose-pink, purple, and bicolor spring radishes for a rainbow at harvest, making it a fun crop for kids. As a spring type it grows best in the cool periods of spring and fall and turns pithy in summer heat. Sow directly and thin seedlings early for well-formed roots. Keep soil evenly moist for crisp, mild radishes. Eat fresh in salads or as a crunchy snack.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once soil has warmed; loves heat.

directions: This heat-loving green offers nutritious, colorful foliage with a hearty spinach flavor that is sweet and slightly tangy. It thrives when spring greens bolt in warm weather and is drought tolerant once established. Use it fresh in salads or steamed, stir-fried, or added to soups. Sow directly into warm soil in full sun. Harvest young leaves regularly to keep plants tender and productive.

planting.note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: More closely related to leeks than true garlic, elephant garlic produces very large bulbs of 1 to 6 mild, tan cloves that are superb roasted. Plant individual cloves in fall, pointed end up, in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch for winter protection. Remove the edible scapes in early summer to direct energy into the bulbs. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure in a dry, airy place. Space plants generously since they grow tall and wide.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once soil is warm.

directions: This zucchini was bred with a more compact, open habit so fruit is easier to spot and harvest before it grows oversized. Plants produce reliably all summer, yielding versatile dark-green squash for countless dishes. Harvest young at 6 to 8 inches and pick often to keep production high. Sow directly into warm, fertile soil in full sun. Water consistently and allow airflow to reduce mildew.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and in late summer; succession sow for a steady supply.

directions: This uniform, short-headed leaf lettuce has little core, so a single cut above the base releases evenly sized leaves for easy salad prep. It is slow to bolt for a long harvest and offers resistance to downy mildew and lettuce aphid. Grow in full sun to partial shade in cool weather, providing afternoon shade in heat. Keep soil consistently moist for tender, sweet leaves. Succession sow every couple of weeks for a continuous supply.

planting.note: Sprout indoors year-round in a jar or tray; no garden bed needed.

directions: Fenugreek sprouts are easy to grow indoors and rich in vitamins A, B6, and C along with iron and potassium. Rinse seeds and soak overnight, then drain and rinse two to three times daily in a sprouting jar or tray. Keep them at room temperature out of direct sun and harvest in about four to seven days when small shoots appear. Enjoy the mildly bitter, nutty sprouts fresh in salads and sandwiches. Refrigerate after harvest to keep them crisp.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost; tolerates heat for a long harvest.

directions: This colorful chard mix produces stems and veins in shades of red, orange, yellow, pink, and white on tender, nutritious leaves. Enjoy baby greens in as little as 20 days or let leaves mature for cooking. It is as ornamental as it is edible and looks at home in vegetable beds, flower borders, and containers. Sow directly and thin seedlings, since each seed is a cluster. Harvest outer leaves regularly and it will keep producing into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant near the last frost.

directions: This Italian heirloom, grown since the late 1800s, forms wine-colored, flattened globes about 1 inch tall and 2 to 3 inches across with a sweet flavor perfect for roasting and grilled kebabs. Start seed indoors early since onions need a long season. Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Keep beds weeded and evenly watered until tops flop over, then cure bulbs in a dry, airy place. Choose day-length appropriate types for your latitude for best bulbing.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall greens.

directions: A long-time Southern favorite, this productive mustard offers big, flavorful leaves for cooked greens and small young leaves for fresh salads. It grows best in cool weather and can bolt quickly in heat, so sow in spring and fall. Direct sow into fertile, moist soil in full sun to partial shade. Thin seedlings and harvest outer leaves as needed. Keep evenly watered for tender, mild greens.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost for a long harvest into fall.

directions: This old favorite features broad, crinkled leaves and wide white stems packed with vitamins and minerals. A vigorous grower with a long season, it provides fresh greens from early summer into fall and year-round in mild climates. White-stemmed types are generally sweeter and milder than colored varieties. Sow directly, thin seedlings since seeds are clusters, and harvest outer leaves regularly. Keep evenly watered for continuous, tender growth.

planting.note: Start indoors early; graft a scion onto it, then transplant after frost.

directions: This vigorous rootstock is grown to be grafted onto a favorite heirloom or hybrid scion to boost its performance, offering better leaf cover to reduce sunscald, more flowers and fruit weight, and improved resistance to soil stresses and diseases. Start rootstock and scion seed together indoors, then graft when stems are pencil-thick. Keep grafted plants in a humid, shaded chamber to heal before hardening off. Transplant after frost into full sun. Support the vigorous vines with sturdy stakes or cages.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and late summer; succession sow for a steady supply.

directions: This 1793 European heirloom romaine has bright green, crisp leaves dappled with striking red freckles and a vase-like habit. The flavor is mildly bitter with a clean finish, and it tolerates heat better than many romaines, resisting bolting. Grow in full sun to partial shade in cool, moist soil, offering afternoon shade in warm spells. Harvest whole heads or pick outer leaves as needed. Succession sow for a steady supply.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall.

directions: A century-old favorite, this oblong spring radish has red shoulders fading to white tips and a delicate, mild flavor. It grows best in the cool weather of spring and fall but tolerates a bit of summer heat. Sow directly and thin seedlings early for well-shaped roots. Keep soil evenly moist so roots stay crisp and mild rather than pithy or hot. It makes a beautiful addition to vegetable platters and is fun for kids to grow.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once soil has warmed; beans dislike cold, wet soil.

directions: These slender French filet beans, or haricots verts, are pencil-thin at about a quarter inch across and 4 inches long, with exceptional tenderness. Pick them young and often for the best texture and to keep the compact bush plants producing. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, since seeds rot in cold, wet ground. Water at the base to keep foliage dry and reduce disease. Steam or saute lightly to preserve their delicate flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: This pungent Chinese mustard, also called Indian mustard or Swatow mustard, forms curled leaves and wide, crunchy stems favored in Chinese, Korean, and Japanese cooking and pickling. The flavor is milder when young and grows sharper with maturity and heat. Grow in cool weather in full sun to partial shade, sowing in spring and fall. Keep soil evenly moist to slow bolting and keep flavor mild. Harvest whole heads or outer leaves as needed.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once soil is warm; needs a long season.

directions: This French heirloom, nicknamed peanut pumpkin for the sugar-crystal warts on its peach-colored skin, is prized for its supremely sweet, velvety orange flesh in soups and sauces. Fruit weighs 10 to 15 pounds and needs a long warm season to mature fully. Sow into rich, well-drained soil in full sun and give vines ample room to sprawl. Water deeply and feed for best size. Harvest when the rind hardens and cure in a warm, dry place before storage.

planting.note: Sprout indoors year-round in a jar or tray; no garden bed required.

directions: Garbanzo bean sprouts are easy to grow indoors and rich in vitamins and protein for snacking or adding to many dishes. Rinse and soak the seeds overnight, then drain and rinse two to three times a day in a sprouting jar or tray. Keep them at room temperature out of direct sun and harvest in just a few days when short sprouts appear. Cook lightly or eat the crisp sprouts fresh. Refrigerate promptly after harvest to keep them fresh.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out after frost danger passes.

directions: This 1950s German heirloom is often praised as one of the best cherry tomatoes, with extra-sweet, flavor-packed fruit borne in clusters of 6 to 12 all summer until frost. The vigorous indeterminate vines easily reach 6 feet or more. Provide tall, sturdy support and prune suckers for airflow and larger clusters. Start seed indoors early and transplant into full sun after frost. Water consistently to prevent cracking and pick often to keep vines producing.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once soil is warm.

directions: This German heirloom pattypan bears vibrant yellow, scalloped fruit with creamy yellow and light-green striping. For the best flavor and texture, harvest squash starting at about 2 inches wide up to 4 inches. Pick young and often to keep the plants producing all season. Sow directly into warm, fertile soil in full sun. Water consistently and allow good airflow to limit powdery mildew.

planting.note: Start indoors for spring, or sow mid-summer for a fall crop; flavor sweetens after frost.

directions: A Southern staple since the 1800s, this productive collard tolerates both heat and cold, with large leaves for steaming or for use as low-calorie wraps. Flavor sweetens after a light frost. Grow in full sun to partial shade in fertile, moist soil and give plants room since they get tall. Harvest lower leaves as needed and the plant keeps producing from the top. Keep evenly watered for tender, mild greens.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall.

directions: This aptly named radish can be pulled small at quarter size or left to grow as large as a tennis ball while staying crisp and flavorful. It is crack-resistant and holds its texture without turning woody or spongy as it matures. Sow directly in cool weather and thin seedlings to give the large roots room. Keep soil evenly moist for the best flavor. Grows well in spring and fall in full sun to partial shade.

planting.note: Plant cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: This porcelain hardneck garlic, also sold as German Extra-Hardy, forms impressive 2 inch or larger bulbs with 4 to 7 fat, easy-peeling cloves and a strong, robust flavor. Plant individual cloves in fall, pointed end up, in fertile, well-drained soil, then mulch for winter protection. Cut the scapes in early summer to channel energy into the bulbs. Harvest when the lower leaves brown and cure in a dry, airy place. It stores well and is very cold-hardy.
